question.text: What is the maximum loss for an option buyer and an option seller?

answer.text: Option buyer: maximum loss is the premium paid (limited). Option seller: maximum loss is theoretically unlimited for naked calls, or strike minus premium for naked puts
Both buyer and seller face unlimited losses
Both buyer and seller have losses limited to the premium amount
The option buyer can lose more than the premium due to margin calls

explanation.text: **The Logic:** Buyer's max loss = premium paid (can't lose more because they simply don't exercise). Seller's max loss: Naked call seller = unlimited (price can rise infinitely). Naked put seller = K - Premium (if underlying goes to zero). This asymmetry is why sellers must deposit margin while buyers don't. **The Trap:** Only sellers face potentially unlimited losses. Buyers never get margin calls (they've already paid the full premium upfront). Both sides don't have symmetric risk. **Cross-Exam Context:** This risk asymmetry is universal across all option markets. It's why option selling requires margin while buying doesn't. **Distributor's Pro-Tip:** Never sell naked options without understanding the worst-case loss — hedge with spreads (bull/bear spreads, iron condors) to cap your maximum loss at a defined amount.
